etymology: Non-Sino-Vietnamese reading of Chinese 朝 (“to meet; to meet a senior person; to attend the emperor's audience”, SV: triều). Doublet of chầu and triều. (Nguyễn Văn Khang. Từ ngoại lai trong tiếng Việt, 2007)
Non-Sino-Vietnamese reading of Chinese 爸 (“father”, SV: bả).
Non-Sino-Vietnamese reading of Chinese 爸 (“father”, SV: bả).
Non-Sino-Vietnamese reading of Chinese 媽 (“mother”, SV: ma).

From Proto-Vietic *meːʔ ~ *mɛːʔ, from Proto-Mon-Khmer *meeʔ ~ *ʔmeeʔ (“mother”) ; cognate with Bahnar mĕ, Khmer ម៉ែ (mae), Mon မိ (mìˀ), Khasi mei. Ultimately a nursery word, compare Old Chinese 母 (*məʔ) (B-S) and Proto-Tai *meːᴮ (whence Thai แม่ (mɛ̂ɛ)). Related to mái.

From Proto-Tai *ɢwɯnᴬ (“human being; person”). Cognate with Northern Thai ᨤᩫ᩠ᨶ, Lao ຄົນ (khon), Lü ᦅᦳᧃ (kun), Shan ၵူၼ်း (kúun), Tai Nüa ᥐᥨᥢᥰ (kön), Ahom 𑜀𑜤𑜃𑜫 (kun), Bouyei wenz, Zhuang vunz, Zuojiang Zhuang koenz. Nôm form of con (“child”).
Sino-Vietnamese word from 婆 (“grandmother”).
Non-Sino-Vietnamese reading of Chinese 油 (“oil”, SV: du).

From Proto-Vietic *mat, from Proto-Mon-Khmer *mat. Akin to Muong [Hòa Bình] mặt , Old Mon mɔt (modern Mon မတ် (mòt)), Bahnar măt, Pacoh mát, Khasi 'mat/khmat, Khmu mat, Mang mat⁷, Car Nicobarese mat, Santali ᱢᱮᱫ (met’).  Compare also Proto-Austronesian *maCa (Malay mata), Proto-Tai *p.taːᴬ (Thai ตา (dtaa)), Proto-Sino-Tibetan *s-mjak ~ s-mik (Chinese 目 (mù < OC *C.muk), Tibetan མིག (mig)).
From Proto-Vietic *muːs (“nose”), from Proto-Mon-Khmer *muh ~ *muuh ~ *muus (“ nose”); cognates include Khmer ច្រមុះ (crɑmoh), Bahnar muh, Khasi khmut, Mon မိုဟ်, Car Nicobarese meh.
From Middle Vietnamese 𥚇 (lưởi), from Proto-Vietic *laːs. Cognate with Nghệ An /Hà Tĩnh dialects lại, Muong lãi. Compare also Pacoh leah (“to lick”).

From Middle Vietnamese tiếng, ultimately from Chinese 聲 (MC ɕiᴇŋ), likely through a Tai language (compare Thai เสียง (sǐiang)) since the tonal correspondence is atypical for a direct borrowing. Cognate with Muong thiểng.
From Middle Vietnamese 𦹵 (cỏ), from Proto-Vietic *kɔh (“grass”). Cognate with Thavung ก็อฮ.

Compare Proto-Katuic *ʔapaal (“shoulder”) (whence Pacoh apal). Thai บ่า  Indonesian Bahu  Some North Central dialects have the form ban with unlenited ‹b› (vs. standard form with lenited ‹v›) and ‹-n› reflex of earlier *-l.

From Proto-Vietic *ɗaːk (“water”), from Proto-Mon-Khmer *ɗaak (“water”). Cognates with Nghệ An/Hà Tĩnh dialects nác, Muong đác, Khmer ទឹក (tik), Bahnar đak, Eastern Mnong dak, Central Nicobarese râk/dâk and Santali ᱫᱟᱜ (dak’). From Proto-Tai *C̬.namᶜ (“water”). Cognate with Northern Thai ᨶ᩶ᩣᩴ (nam), Southern Thai หน่าม, Lao ນ້ຳ (nam), Lü ᦓᧄᧉ (nam2), Tai Dam ꪙꪾ꫁ (nam6), Shan ၼမ်ႉ (nâ̰m), Tai Nüa ᥘᥛᥳ (lam5), Khamti ꩫမ်ႇ, Aiton ꩫံ (naṃ), Phake ꩫံ (naṃ), Ahom 𑜃𑜪 (naṃ), Bouyei ramx, Zhuang raemx, Nong Zhuang naemx, Zuojiang Zhuang naemx, Saek นัม. Compare Proto-Be *namᴮᶜ², Proto-Hlai *C-nəmʔ.

From Proto-Vietic *ʔa-cɔːʔ, from Proto-Mon-Khmer *cɔʔ (“dog”); cognate with Semai co (“dog, servant, slave”), Halang chô (“dog”), Pacoh acho (“dog”), Khmu sɔʔ and Mang θɔː¹. Compare also Khmer ច (cɑɑ, “Year of the Dog”), a borrowing from Vietic.
From Proto-Vietic *poːŋ (“flower”). Cognate with Muong pông and Thavung โปง.
Sinitic

From Middle Vietnamese ſao, from Proto-Vietic *k-raːw. Cognate with Muong khao. Might represent a Kra-Dai or Austronesian substratum, compare Proto-Tai *t.naːwᴬ (“star”), Proto-Hlai *ɾaːw (“star”) and Proto-Austronesian *qajaw (“ day”).  It is a semantic loan from English star, in the senses of “celebrity; actor”.
Sinitic
From Middle Vietnamese 𥚇 (lưởi), from Proto-Vietic *laːs. Cognate with Nghệ An /Hà Tĩnh dialects lại, Muong lãi. Compare also Pacoh leah (“to lick”).
From Proto-Vietic *-naŋʔ.
From Proto-Vietic *ɓəːjʔ. Unrelated to Thai ใหม่ (mài) or Khmer ថ្មី (thməy).

From Middle Vietnamese già, from Proto-Vietic *k-raː, from Proto-Mon-Khmer *kraʔ ~ *(k)rah. Cognate with Muong khà, Tho [Cuối Chăm] kʰraː¹, Bahnar kră. Compare also Nghệ An/Hà Tĩnh dialects tra.  The standard written Vietnamese form is irregular (the expected form would be **sa), while the North Central form shows the shift from *k-r to *k-l observable in several other instances: compare sâu and trâu (“caterpillar”), sừng and trừng (“horn”). See giòi (“maggot”) for another instance of ‹gi› from initial *k-r.
Non-Sino-Vietnamese reading of Chinese 古 (“old”, SV: cổ). Doublet of cổ.
Non-Sino-Vietnamese reading of Chinese 望 (“to expect”, SV: vọng). Compare Thai มอง (mɔɔng).
From Proto-Vietic *pər, from Proto-Mon-Khmer *par; cognates include Muong păl, Bahnar păr, Pacoh pár and Mon ပဝ် (pɔ).

From Proto-Southwestern Tai *kʰwiːᴮ (“to ride”), from Middle Chinese 騎 (MC ɡˠiᴇ, “to ride”).[1] Cognate with Lao ຂີ່ (khī), Lü ᦃᦲᧈ (ẋii1), Tai Dam ꪄꪲ꪿, Shan ၶီႇ (khìi), Ahom 𑜁𑜣 (khī), Zhuang gwih, Nong Zhuang kiq.

Cognate with Arem anam ("to brood").  Compare Proto-Katuic *-nam (whence Pacoh panám (“to set on eggs, to brood”)) and Proto-Khmuic *gnəm (“to hatch or brood eggs”). to lie (be in a horizontal position on a surface)
From Proto-Vietic *goːt. Cognate with Chut [Sách] koːt⁸.
Short for ngáo đá (“be high/intoxicated after using     methamphetamine”).  2. (colloquial) acting stupidly
From Middle Chinese 炭 (MC tʰɑnH, “charcoal”). Cognate with Northern Thai ᨳ᩵ᩣ᩠ᨶ, Lao ຖ່ານ (thān), Shan ထၢၼ်ႇ (thàan).
From Middle Chinese 炭 (MC tʰɑnH, “charcoal”). Cognate with Northern Thai ᨳ᩵ᩣ᩠ᨶ, Lao ຖ່ານ (thān), Shan ထၢၼ်ႇ (thàan).
From Proto-Vietic *roːŋʔ. Cognate with Northern Thai ᩁ᩵ᩬᨦ, Lao ຮ່ອງ (hǭng), Khün ᩁ᩵ᩬᨦ, Shan ႁွင်ႈ (hōang), Ahom 𑜍𑜨𑜂𑜫 (roṅ).
Non-Sino-Vietnamese reading of Chinese 幔 (SV: mạn).  Cognate with Northern Thai ᨾ᩵ᩣ᩠ᨶ, Lao ມ່ານ (mān), Khün ᨾ᩵ᩣ᩠ᨶ, Lü ᦙᦱᧃᧈ (maan1), Shan မၢၼ်ႈ (māan).
From Proto-Mon-Khmer *luh (“hole”); cognates include Khmer ធ្លុះ (thluh), Bahnar bơlôh, Central Mnong lôh, and proto-Palaungic *luuh.
From Proto-Vietic *diː ~ tiː (“to go, to walk”); cognate with Muong ti, Chut [Rục] tiː², Thavung tiː² and Tho [Cuối Chăm] tiː².

From Proto-Southwestern Tai *ʔaːŋᴮ¹ (“basin”), from Proto-Tai *ʔaːŋᴮ (“basin”), from Middle Chinese 盎 (MC ʔɑŋX, ʔɑŋH, “an earthen vessel with a big belly and a small mouth”). Cognate with Northern Thai ᩋ᩵ᩣ᩠ᨦ, Lao ອ່າງ (ʼāng), Tai Dam ꪮ꪿ꪱꪉ, Shan ဢၢင်ႇ (ʼàang), Tai Nüa ᥟᥣᥒᥱ (ʼǎang), Ahom 𑜒𑜂𑜫 (ʼaṅ).
From Proto-Mon-Khmer *wiŋ ~ *wiiŋ ~ *wiəŋ ~ *waiŋ ~ *wəŋ ~ *wuəŋ ~ *wuŋ ~ *wuuŋ (“to go round; to turn”); cognate with Khmer វង់ (vŭəng, “circle”). Related to vồng, quầng, vầng.
Non-Sino-Vietnamese reading of Chinese 兔 (SV: thố).
From Middle Vietnamese căóc, cóc.

From Proto-Tai *ʰlaːjᴬ (“many; much”), from Old Chinese 多 (OC *[t.l]ˤaj) (B-S). Cognate with Northern Thai ᩉ᩠ᩃᩣ᩠ᨿ, Lao ຫຼາຍ (lāi), Lü ᦜᦻ (l̇aay), Khün ᩉ᩠ᩃᩣ᩠ᨿ, Shan လၢႆ (lǎay), Bouyei laail, Zhuang lai, Saek หล่าย. Compare Jizhao laːi²¹.

From Proto-Vietic *buːŋʔ, from Proto-Mon-Khmer *buŋ() ~ *buuŋʔ. Cognate with Khmer ពុង (pung), Mon ဗုၚ် (pɜ̀ŋ), Thai พุง (pung).  In Vietnamese culture, instead of "heart", traditionally "belly, abdomen" was considered to be where people harbor their feelings and emotions. See also lòng (“intestine”) and dạ (“belly, stomach”).

From Proto-Vietic *ɓaːk (“to carry (on shoulder)”) (though initial is irregular). Cognate with Northern Thai ᨷᩯ᩠ᨠ, Lao ແບກ (bǣk), Tai Dam ꪵꪚꪀ, Shan မႅၵ်ႇ (màek) or ဝႅၵ်ႇ (wàek), Ahom 𑜉𑜢𑜀𑜫 (mik), Nong Zhuang mbeg.
Cognate with Lao ບີບ (bīp), Tai Dam ꪚꪲꪚ, Shan မိပ်ႇ (mìp) or ဝိပ်ႇ (wìp).
(archaic or dialectal, Central Vietnam) Alternative form of đường (“road”)       □ 1944, Lưu Hữu Phước (lyrics and music), “Lên đàng [Hit the Road]”:

From Proto-Vietic *cih (“to point”); probably related to Sino-Vietnamese word from 指 (“finger”). From Proto-Tai *ɟiːᶜ (“to point”), from Middle Chinese 指 (MC t͡ɕˠiɪX, “finger; to point; to indicate”). Cognate with Northern Thai ᨩᩦ᩶, Lao ຊີ້ (sī), Lü ᦋᦲᧉ (tsii2), Tai Dam ꪋꪲ꫁, Shan ၸီႉ (tsḭ̂i), Ahom 𑜋𑜣 (chī), Nong Zhuang jix. Compare Vietnamese chỉ, chĩa.

From Proto-Vietic *raːwʔ; cognate with Muong rão, Tho [Cuối Chăm] /riɛw⁴/, Proto-Pong /*riɛw⁴/. Compare Proto-Tai *ʰlawᶜ (whence Thai เหล้า (lâo)), Chinese 醪 (OC *ruːw). From Proto-Tai *ʰlawᶜ. Cognate with Northern Thai ᩉᩖᩮᩢ᩶ᩣ, Lao ເຫຼົ້າ (lao), Lü ᦜᧁᧉ (l̇aw2), Tai Dam ꪹꪨ꫁ꪱ, Shan လဝ်ႈ (lāw), Tai Nüa ᥘᥝᥲ (làw), Phake လွ် (law), Ahom 𑜎𑜨𑜧 (low), Bouyei lauc, Zhuang laeuj, Saek เล̂า. Compare Middle Chinese 醪 (MC lɑu, “liquor”).
From Proto-Vietic *r-kaː. Cognate with Thavung กา. Compare Khmer រកា (rɔkaa, “ Year of the Rooster”).
Non-Sino-Vietnamese reading of Chinese 膠 (“glue”, SV: giao), unattested. Cognate with Thai กาว (gaao), Lao ກາວ (kāo), Burmese ကော် (kau), Khmer កាវ (kaaw).

From Proto-Southwestern Tai *mɛːwᴬ² (“cat”), of onomatopoeic origin. Cognate with Northern Thai ᨾᩯ᩠ᩅ, Lao ແມວ (mǣu), Lü ᦶᦙᧁ (maew), Tai Dam ꪵꪣꪫ, Shan မႅဝ်း (máew), Tai Nüa ᥛᥦᥝᥴ (máew), Phake မိဝ် (miw), Ahom 𑜈𑜢𑜈𑜫 (biw). Compare Zhuang meuz, Chinese 貓, Vietnamese mèo, Khasi miaw.

From Proto-Tai *ˀjeːwᴬ (“waist”), from Middle Chinese 腰 (MC ʔiᴇu, “waist”). Cognate with Northern Thai ᩋᩯ᩠ᩅ, Lao ແອວ (ʼǣu), Tai Dam ꪵꪮꪫ, Shan ဢႅဝ် (ʼǎew), Zhuang yiu. Possibly related to เอี้ยว (îao, “to twist, to bend (one's body)”).
French 'bread' From Old Occitan, from Vulgar Latin *baneum, from Latin balneum.
From Proto-Mon-Khmer *ŋǝm ~ *ŋam (“to immerse, soak”); cognate with Muong ngâm, Khasi ngam (“to dive into”).

From Proto-Vietic *ɓǝt; cognate with Arem bʉt. Cognate with Northern Thai ᩉ᩠ᨾᩫᨯ, Lao ໝົດ (mot), Lü ᦷᦖᧆ (ṁod), Shan မူတ်း (múut). Compare Middle Chinese 歿 (MC muət̚) 歿 to die Alternative form of 沒／没. to sink, to have no.

From Proto-Vietic *p-laːŋʔ (“bright”) (in which *pl- had changed to *pʰr- and from there developed to its current form), from Proto-Mon-Khmer *plaaŋʔ. Cognates with láng (in sáng láng), Tho [Cuối Chăm] blaːŋ³. Compare also Thai ปลั่ง (bplàng).  Cognate with Old Khmer សេង (sēṅa, seṅ, “radiance, brilliance; luster, gleam; etc”), modern Khmer សែង (saeng), Northern Thai ᩈᩯ᩠ᨦ, Lao ແສງ (sǣng), Lü ᦶᦉᧂ (ṡaeng), Ahom 𑜏𑜢𑜂𑜫 (siṅ).
Sino-Vietnamese word from 勃 (“fine powder”), from an obsolete reading.
From Proto-Vietic *p-ɗuŋʔ ~ *p-ruŋʔ (“hot”).
From Middle Vietnamese tlao᷄, from Proto-Vietic *k-lɔːŋ (“clear; limpid”).
Sino-Vietnamese word from 臆 (“chest”). Compare Proto-Tai *ʔɤkᴰ (“chest”), whence Thai อก (òk).
From Proto-Vietic *k-ləŋ (“back”). Compare Proto-Tai *ʰlaŋᴬ (“back”), whence Thai หลัง (lǎng).
From Proto-Tai *boːŋᴬ. Cognate with Lao ພອງ (phǭng) or ໂພງ (phōng), Shan ပွင်း (póang). Possibly related to โปง (bpoong), โป่ง (bpòong), โพรง (proong).

From Proto-Vietic *k-laːjʔ, from Proto-Mon-Khmer *klaj(ʔ) ~ *klaaj(ʔ) (“to change”), cognate with Muong tlải, Khmer ក្លាយ (klaay, “to change”), Mon ကၠဲာ (klai , “to translate”). Compare also Thai กลาย (glaai), Lao ກາຍ (kāi). From Old Chinese 左 (OC *ʔsaːlʔ, *ʔsaːls). Cognate with Lao ຊ້າຍ (sāi), Shan သၢႆႉ (sâ̰ay), Ahom 𑜏𑜩 (say), Bouyei soix, Zhuang swix.

From Proto-Vietic *k-moːlʔ ~ *k-muːrʔ ~ *k-moːrʔ. Cognate with Proto-Katuic *kmuar (whence Pacoh camoar (“termite”)), Semai kemur (“caterpillar, maggot”), Khmer ខ្មើរ (khmaə, “flying ant”), ខ្មុល (khmol, “mole cricket”).
Cognate with Tho [Cuối Chăm] pʰrɛːk⁸.  Pacoh prec is likely a Vietic loan, due to the lack of cognates in other Katuic languages.
From Proto-Vietic *kaɲʔ.
Sino-Vietnamese word from 過 (“pass”). Doublet of quá.

From Middle Vietnamese 𤝞 (chuọt, “mouse”), from Proto-Vietic *k-ɟɔːt (“ squirrel”). Compare Chinese 鼠 and Japanese 鼠 (nezumi), both of which also refer to generic mouse or rat-like animals. Also compare Latin mūsculus (“ muscle”, literally “little mouse”) and Ancient Greek μῦς (mûs, “mouse; muscle”) .
1. (chiefly Northern Vietnam) sentence-ending intensifying particle          Synonym: đó

From Sinitic 在 From Proto-Tai *diːᴮ. Cognate with Lao ທີ່ (thī), Northern Thai ᨴᩦ᩵, Lü ᦑᦲᧈ (tii1), Shan တီႈ (tīi), Ahom 𑜄𑜣 (tī), Zhuang dih. Outside Tai-Kadai: compare Proto-Mon-Khmer *tiʔ ~ tiiʔ ~ tiəʔ (“that yonder”), Mon တေံ (teʔ, “that”), Khmer ទី (tii), Vietnamese thì (“then, which, to be”). Compare Chinese 地 (MC diɪH), Malay di.

From Proto-Vietic *ʔəh (“to stay”); cognate with Muong ớ.  Most prepositions in Vietnamese were originally verbs: cho (“to give; for”), đến (“to come; to”), tới (“to arrive; to”), lên (“to go up; onto”), vào (“to enter; in, into”), theo (“to follow; according to”), về (“to return; about”).  Similarity to Chinese 於 is coincidental, as semantically the Vietnamese word is a grammaticalized verbal and phonetically there is a tonal mismatch. From Proto-Tai *ˀjuːᴮ (“to be (in a place)”). Cognate with Northern Thai ᩀᩪ᩵, Lao ຢູ່ (yū), Lü ᦊᦴᧈ (ẏuu1), Tai Dam ꪤꪴ꪿, Shan ယူႇ (yùu), Ahom 𑜊𑜥 (jʸū), Bouyei qyus, Zhuang youq.
